schema_notes: Fourth Rashidun caliph. Cousin and son-in-law of the Prophet, raised in his household from childhood. Present at every major event of the sirah except Tabuk. His caliphate was consumed by the First Fitna, with major battles at Basra (Camel), Siffin on the Euphrates, and Nahrawan east of the Tigris. Assassinated while praying in Kufa.
sources: Ibn Ishaq, Sirat Rasul Allah (Guillaume tr.)
Wilferd Madelung, The Succession to Muhammad

journey.event: Born in Mecca to Abu Talib, the Prophet's uncle and protector, and Fatima bint Asad. Tradition holds he was born inside the Kaaba itself — a claim unique to him in Islamic history.
Taken into the household of the Prophet as a young child after a famine strained Abu Talib's means. Raised by the Prophet and Khadija.
Accepted Islam while still a child — by tradition the first male to believe after Khadija. Prayed alongside the Prophet in the valleys of Mecca before the message was made public.
At the Feast of Dhu al-Ashira, when the Prophet first called his wider kin to Islam, only Ali — a boy — stood up to pledge his support.

On the night of the Hijra, slept in the Prophet's bed wrapped in his green Hadrami cloak to deceive the Quraysh assassins surrounding the house. Remained in Mecca for several more days afterwards to return goods the Prophet had been holding in trust for their owners.
Caught up with the Prophet at Quba after walking from Mecca. His feet were badly swollen from the journey.
Entered Medina. Settled in the new community.
Married the Prophet's youngest daughter Fatima — a marriage that would produce Hasan, Husayn, and the line that Shi'a tradition traces as the Prophet's family, Ahl al-Bayt.
Fought at Badr as one of three Muslim champions in the opening duels. Killed Walid ibn Utba and, with Hamza, dispatched Utba ibn Rabi'a. Carried the Muhajirun standard.
Fought at Uhud. Held his ground with the small group around the wounded Prophet when most of the army broke.
Killed the Quraysh champion Amr ibn Abd Wadd in single combat during the siege — a duel celebrated in later tradition as worth more than all the worship of the community combined.

Wrote down the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah on the Prophet's dictation. When the Quraysh negotiator objected to the phrase 'Muhammad the Messenger of God', the Prophet asked Ali to erase it; Ali refused, and the Prophet erased it himself.

At Khaybar, took the great fortress of Qamus after others had failed. Tradition says the Prophet gave him the standard saying, 'I will give the banner tomorrow to a man who loves God and His Messenger, and whom God and His Messenger love'. Earned the title Asadullah — Lion of God.
Participated in the conquest of Mecca.
Fought at Hunayn, one of the small group who remained firm around the Prophet when the army broke in the initial ambush.
Sent by the Prophet to Yemen as judge and envoy — a mission that took him through the tribes of Najran and beyond, establishing Muslim governance in the south.
Caught up with Abu Bakr on the route to the Hajj of year 9, carrying the Prophet's instructions and the newly-revealed opening verses of Surah al-Tawbah to proclaim at Mina.
Present at the Farewell Pilgrimage.

On the Prophet's death, washed and prepared his body for burial with a few close relatives while the Saqifa meeting chose Abu Bakr. Did not attend the Saqifa; the question of succession would shape the rest of his life and the Shi'a-Sunni divide afterwards.

Lived in Medina through the caliphates of Abu Bakr, Umar, and Uthman — consulted as a senior advisor and judge, particularly on questions of law and the Quran. Remembered as one of the most learned of the companions.

Acclaimed as the fourth caliph in Medina after the rebels killed Uthman. Accepted reluctantly and only after public pledges at the Prophet's Mosque. Some senior companions refused to give him the pledge.
Dismissed several of Uthman's provincial governors. Mu'awiya of Syria refused to be removed, demanding justice for Uthman first.
Marched from Medina with a small force when A'isha, Talha, and Zubayr seized Basra demanding justice for Uthman. Raised additional troops from Kufa en route.

Battle of the Camel, fought outside Basra. Defeated the opposing coalition after several defections. Talha and Zubayr were killed; A'isha, around whose camel the battle raged, was sent safely back to Medina with honour. The first battle between Muslims.

Entered Kufa and made it the capital of the caliphate, refusing to stay in the governor's palace. Kufa would be his base for the rest of his caliphate; Medina permanently lost its political centrality in this move.
Marched his Iraqi army north along the Euphrates to Siffin, near the modern Syrian-Iraqi border, to confront Mu'awiya's Syrian forces. A hundred days of skirmishes and negotiations.

The main battle of Siffin. Ali's forces were close to victory when Mu'awiya's men raised copies of the Quran on their spears, calling for arbitration. Under pressure from his own troops, Ali reluctantly agreed. The arbitration agreement was signed 2 August 657.
Returned to Kufa. A large faction of his army rejected the arbitration as sinful — they would become the Kharijites — and broke away at Harura on the outskirts of the city.

Confronted the Kharijites at the Nahrawan canal east of the Tigris after they began killing civilians and his own messengers. Offered amnesty; about 1,200 accepted. Defeated the remaining ~2,800 in battle. The slaughter of former allies, many of them devout Quran reciters, permanently damaged his support.
Returned to Kufa. Tried to raise a new campaign against Mu'awiya but the Kufan troops, exhausted and disillusioned, refused to march. Mu'awiya steadily expanded his authority through Egypt and Hijaz.

Struck by a poison-coated sword of the Kharijite Ibn Muljam while leading the dawn prayer at the Great Mosque of Kufa on 26 January 661 (19 Ramadan 40 AH). Died two days later on 28 January. His burial place was kept secret; Najaf, where the present shrine stands, is the traditional site. With his death, the Rashidun caliphate ended and Mu'awiya founded the Umayyad dynasty.

confidence.disclosure: Ali's life is documented in al-Tabari, Ibn Sa'd, and Nasr ibn Muzahim's Siffin. Major events (Hijra, battles, caliphate, assassination) have month-level dating. His movements between Medina, Kufa, Basra, and Siffin are well attested.
